title: Fundamental particles
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: This lesson delves into the Standard Model of particle physics, classifying fundamental particles into leptons and quarks. It explores the composition of hadrons (baryons and mesons) from quarks and teaches how to determine the quark make-up of protons, neutrons, and other particles.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between fundamental particles (leptons, quarks) and composite particles (hadrons).
Describe the properties of up, down, and strange quarks, including their charge.
Determine the quark composition of protons and neutrons.
Classify hadrons into baryons (three quarks) and mesons (quark-antiquark pair).

simpleExplanation.title: The Ultimate LEGO Set
simpleExplanation.summary: Everything around us is made from a small set of indivisible 'fundamental' particles, just like a complex model is built from basic LEGO bricks. We classify these particles into two families: leptons and quarks.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you have a special set of LEGO bricks. Some bricks, like the electron, are simple, single pieces – these are the 'leptons'. Other, more complex-looking structures like protons and neutrons are actually built by sticking three smaller, more fundamental bricks together – these are the 'quarks'. You can't break the leptons or quarks down any further; they are the ultimate building blocks of the universe.
steps: Fundamental particles are the universe's most basic, indivisible components. They are grouped into Leptons and Quarks.
Leptons (e.g., electrons, neutrinos) are truly fundamental and do not experience the strong nuclear force.
Hadrons (e.g., protons, neutrons) are composite particles made of quarks. They are divided into Baryons (3 quarks) and Mesons (quark-antiquark).
Protons (uud) and neutrons (udd) are baryons. Their overall charge is found by summing the fractional charges of their constituent up (+2/3 e) and down (-1/3 e) quarks.

flashcards.back: A particle that cannot be broken down into smaller components. Examples include leptons (like electrons) and quarks.

flashcards.front: What are the two main classes of particles in the Standard Model?
flashcards.back: Leptons and Hadrons. Note: Hadrons are NOT fundamental, they are made of quarks.
A fundamental particle that does not feel the strong nuclear force. The electron and neutrino are key examples.
A composite particle made of quarks, held together by the strong nuclear force. Protons and neutrons are hadrons.

flashcards.front: What are the two types of Hadron?
flashcards.back: Baryons (composed of three quarks) and Mesons (composed of one quark and one antiquark).

flashcards.front: Is a proton a fundamental particle?
flashcards.back: No. A proton is a hadron (specifically, a baryon) and is composed of three quarks (uud).

flashcards.front: What is the quark composition of a proton?
flashcards.back: Two up quarks and one down quark (uud).

flashcards.front: What is the quark composition of a neutron?
flashcards.back: One up quark and two down quarks (udd).

flashcards.front: What is the electric charge of an up quark?
flashcards.back: +2/3 e, where 'e' is the elementary charge.

flashcards.front: What is the electric charge of a down quark?
flashcards.back: -1/3 e, where 'e' is the elementary charge.

flashcards.front: What is the electric charge of a strange quark?
flashcards.back: -1/3 e (the same as a down quark).

flashcards.front: How can you remember the composition of Baryons vs Mesons?
flashcards.back: Baryons are made of 3 quarks (think 'Baryons are Big'). Mesons are made of a quark-antiquark pair (think 'Mesons are in the Middle').
A particle that is not composed of any smaller particles and is believed to have no internal structure. The two main types of fundamental matter particles are quarks and leptons.

flashcards.front: State the quark composition of a proton and show how it results in a charge of +e.
flashcards.back:
Composition: two up quarks, one down quark (uud). 
Calculation: (+2/3e) + (+2/3e) + (-1/3e) = +3/3e = +e.

flashcards.front: State the quark composition of a neutron and show how it results in a charge of 0.
flashcards.back:
Composition: one up quark, two down quarks (udd). 
Calculation: (+2/3e) + (-1/3e) + (-1/3e) = 0.

flashcards.front: How do baryons and mesons differ in their quark structure?
flashcards.back: Baryons are composed of three quarks (qqq). Mesons are composed of one quark and one antiquark (qq̄). Both are types of hadron.

flashcards.front: What are the electric charges of the up, down, and strange quarks?

sections.content: In previous topics, we treated protons, neutrons, and electrons as the basic building blocks of atoms. Now, we zoom in even further. Particle physics reveals that some of these particles are themselves made of even smaller, more 'fundamental' constituents. This topic introduces the 'Standard Model', our current best description of the particle zoo and the forces that govern it.
The Standard Model and Fundamental Particles

The Standard Model of particle physics is our most complete theory describing the fundamental particles and the forces that govern their interactions. A fundamental particle is one that has no internal structure and cannot be broken down into smaller components. All matter in the universe is composed of these particles. They are broadly classified into two families: quarks and leptons. Protons and neutrons, which form atomic nuclei, are not fundamental. Instead, they are composite particles, known as hadrons, built from quarks. Understanding this classification is the first step to unravelling the structure of matter at the most basic level. The Standard Model successfully explains a vast range of experimental results and provides a coherent framework for particle physics.

items: Fundamental particles are the ultimate building blocks of matter, with no known internal structure.
The Standard Model classifies fundamental matter particles into quarks and leptons.
Particles like protons and neutrons are composite, not fundamental.
Quarks and leptons differ primarily in their interaction with the strong nuclear force.

sections.content: Leptons are a class of fundamental particles defined by the fact that they do not experience the strong nuclear force. The most familiar lepton is the electron, a key component of every atom. Another important lepton is the electron neutrino, which is produced in nuclear processes like beta decay. Leptons have integer charges; the electron has a charge of -e, while the neutrino is electrically neutral. For every lepton, there exists a corresponding antiparticle with the same mass but opposite charge and other quantum numbers. For example, the antiparticle of the electron is the positron (charge +e), and the antiparticle of the electron neutrino is the electron antineutrino. Leptons can exist as free, isolated particles.

items: Leptons are fundamental particles that do not interact via the strong nuclear force.
The syllabus focuses on the electron (charge -e) and the electron neutrino (charge 0).
Every lepton has an antiparticle, such as the positron (e+).
Leptons can be observed individually, unlike quarks.

sections.content: Quarks are the second class of fundamental particles. Unlike leptons, they experience the strong nuclear force. A peculiar property of quarks is that they have fractional electric charges. The A-Level syllabus requires you to know three 'flavours' of quark: the up quark (u) with a charge of +2/3e, the down quark (d) with a charge of -1/3e, and the strange quark (s) with a charge of -1/3e. Due to a phenomenon called 'quark confinement', quarks are never observed in isolation. They are always bound together by the strong force to form composite particles called hadrons. Every quark also has a corresponding antiquark (ū, đ, s̄) with the same mass but opposite charge.

items: Quarks are fundamental particles that experience the strong nuclear force.
They have fractional charges: up (u) is +2/3e, down (d) is -1/3e, strange (s) is -1/3e.
Quarks are confined within composite particles called hadrons and cannot exist alone.
Antiquarks have the opposite charge to their corresponding quark (e.g., anti-up (ū) is -2/3e).

sections.content: Hadrons are subdivided into two groups: baryons and mesons. Baryons are composite particles made from a combination of three quarks (qqq). The most stable and common baryons are the proton and the neutron. A proton's composition is 'up, up, down' (uud), giving a total charge of (+2/3 + 2/3 - 1/3)e = +1e. A neutron's composition is 'up, down, down' (udd), resulting in a total charge of (+2/3 - 1/3 - 1/3)e = 0. Mesons, on the other hand, are made from a quark-antiquark pair (qq̄). They are typically unstable. For example, a positive pion (π+) consists of an up quark and an anti-down quark (ud̄), giving it a charge of (+2/3 - (-1/3))e = +1e.

items: Hadrons are particles made of quarks.
Baryons are made of three quarks (qqq). Examples: proton (uud), neutron (udd).
Mesons are made of one quark and one antiquark (qq̄). Example: pion (ud̄).
The charge of a hadron is the sum of the charges of its constituent quarks.

sections.content: In exam questions, you will often be asked to deduce the quark composition of a hadron given its charge and whether it is a baryon or a meson. Memorise the charges of the u, d, and s quarks. Systematically test combinations of three quarks (for a baryon) or a quark-antiquark pair (for a meson) until you find the combination that yields the correct total charge.
All particles can be sorted into two primary families: leptons and hadrons. The key difference lies in what they are made of and which forces they experience.

items: **Leptons:** These are truly fundamental particles. They do not experience the strong nuclear force. The most familiar lepton is the **electron** ($e^-$). Another important lepton is the **neutrino** ($\nu$), which has almost no mass and no charge.

**Hadrons:** These are composite particles, meaning they are made of smaller parts. They are affected by the strong nuclear force. The familiar **proton** ($p$) and **neutron** ($n$) are both hadrons.

sections.content: Hadrons are not fundamental because they are all composed of particles called **quarks**. Quarks are fundamental particles, just like leptons. They have the unusual property of possessing fractional electric charges. For A-Level, you need to know about three 'flavours' of quark: up, down, and strange.

items: **Up quark (u):** Charge = $+ \frac{2}{3} e$
**Down quark (d):** Charge = $- \frac{1}{3} e$
**Strange quark (s):** Charge = $- \frac{1}{3} e$

sections.content: Hadrons themselves are subdivided into two groups based on their quark composition:

items: **Baryons:** Composed of **three quarks** (qqq). Protons and neutrons are baryons.
**Mesons:** Composed of **one quark and one antiquark** ($q\bar{q}$). Mesons are generally unstable. An antiquark has the same mass but opposite charge to its corresponding quark.

sections.question: Show that the quark composition 'uud' results in a particle with the properties of a proton (charge $+e$).
sections.solution:
A proton is a baryon with charge $+e$. We need to combine three quarks to form it.

1.  **Identify quark charges:**
    *   Up quark (u) has charge $+ \frac{2}{3} e$.
    *   Down quark (d) has charge $- \frac{1}{3} e$.

2.  **Sum the charges for the 'uud' combination:**
    *   Total charge = (charge of u) + (charge of u) + (charge of d)
    *   Total charge = $(+ \frac{2}{3} e) + (+ \frac{2}{3} e) + (- \frac{1}{3} e)$
    *   Total charge = $(+ \frac{4}{3} e) - (\frac{1}{3} e) = + \frac{3}{3} e$

3.  **Conclusion:**
    *   Total charge = $+1e$.
    *   This matches the charge of a proton. The composition 'uud' consists of three quarks, correctly identifying it as a baryon. [3 marks]

sections.content: A very common exam question asks for the quark composition of a proton (uud) or a neutron (udd). Memorise these two combinations. For any other particle, you will be given enough information (like its total charge, and whether it's a baryon or meson) to deduce the composition. Always write down the charges of the individual quarks and sum them clearly to show your working.

Our understanding of quarks helps explain nuclear processes like beta decay. In beta-minus decay, a neutron turns into a proton. At the fundamental level, this isn't a simple transformation. Instead, a **down quark** within the neutron changes into an **up quark**, emitting an electron and an antineutrino in the process. This changes the neutron (udd) into a proton (uud).

sections.question: The sigma-minus particle ($\Sigma^-$) is a baryon with a charge of $-1e$ and contains one strange quark. Deduce its quark composition.
sections.solution:
1.  **Analyse the particle:**
    *   It is a baryon, so it is made of three quarks (qqq).
    *   Its total charge is $-1e$.
    *   It contains one strange quark (s).

2.  **Account for the strange quark:**
    *   The charge of a strange quark (s) is $- \frac{1}{3} e$.
    *   The remaining two quarks must provide a charge of: $(-1e) - (- \frac{1}{3} e) = - \frac{2}{3} e$.

3.  **Determine the remaining quarks:**
    *   We need to combine two quarks (from up, u, and down, d) to get a total charge of $- \frac{2}{3} e$.
    *   The only combination that works is two down quarks: (charge of d) + (charge of d) = $(- \frac{1}{3} e) + (- \frac{1}{3} e) = - \frac{2}{3} e$.

4.  **State the final composition:**
    *   The quark composition of the $\Sigma^-$ particle is **dds**. [3 marks]

sections.a: For every type of particle, there is a corresponding antiparticle with the same mass but opposite charge and other quantum numbers. For example, the antiparticle of an electron ($e^-$) is a positron ($e^+$). The antiparticle of an up quark (u, charge +2/3 e) is an anti-up quark ($\bar{u}$, charge -2/3 e).

sections.q: Why are quarks always found inside hadrons and never on their own?
sections.a: This is due to a phenomenon called 'quark confinement'. The strong nuclear force that binds quarks together actually gets stronger as you try to pull them apart. The energy required to separate two quarks is so immense that it is more energetically favourable to create a new quark-antiquark pair from the energy, resulting in new hadrons instead of an isolated quark.

sections.q: What's the difference between a hadron and a baryon?
sections.a: Hadron is the main family name for any particle made of quarks. A baryon is a specific *type* of hadron, one that is made of three quarks. The other type of hadron is a meson, made of a quark and an antiquark. So, all baryons are hadrons, but not all hadrons are baryons.

sections.label: Test Your Knowledge on Fundamental Particles

faq.q: If quarks have fractional charges, why do we only ever observe particles with integer charges (like +e or 0)?
faq.a: This is due to 'quark confinement'. Quarks are never found in isolation; they are always bound together inside hadrons. The allowed combinations (three quarks for a baryon, or a quark-antiquark pair for a meson) always result in a total charge that is an integer multiple of the elementary charge, e (e.g., -1e, 0, +1e).

faq.q: Are electrons a type of quark?
faq.a: No, electrons are a completely different type of fundamental particle called a lepton. The key difference is that quarks experience the strong nuclear force, which binds them together inside protons and neutrons, whereas leptons do not. Electrons interact via the electromagnetic and weak nuclear forces.

faq.q: What is an antiparticle? Is it just a particle with an opposite charge?
faq.a: An antiparticle has the same mass as its corresponding particle but has the opposite charge and other quantum numbers (like baryon number). For example, the positron (antielectron) has the same mass as an electron but a charge of +e. For a neutral particle like the neutron (udd), its antiparticle, the antineutron (ūđđ), is also neutral but is composed of antiquarks and has an opposite baryon number.

cells: Integer multiple of e (e.g., -1e for electron, 0 for neutrino)
Fractional multiple of e (e.g., +2/3e for up, -1/3e for down)
